title: Thrombocytopenia -- Treatment / Management
content: Thrombocytopenic emergencies that require immediate action includes conditions of suspected HIT, TTP, HUS, drug-induced ITP, pregnancy with severe thrombocytopenia, bleeding with severe thrombocytopenia, urgently needed an invasive procedure in the presence of severe thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, and aplastic anemia. In patients with bleeding and severe thrombocytopenia, treatment includes platelet transfusion. Management includes identifying the underlying cause and treating it.

title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
content: The vast majority of children recover spontaneously without sequelae. In severe cases, the treatment of choice is intravenous immunoglobulin (IV IgG). A 5-day course of 400 mg/kg/d is given. Responses occur in more than 70% in 1 to 4 days, but only for a short period; many patients respond, and repeated courses may be necessary.

title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
content: Indication for active therapy is only when there is acute bleeding or if emergent surgery is forthcoming. If there is no significant bleeding and the platelet count is > 30,000 /dL, then transfusion may be withheld. [3] In a recent analysis, severe bleeding was seen in about 20% of children and about 10% of adults. Bleeding usually does not occur until the platelet count decreases to below 30,000 /dL. Cutaneous and mucosal bleeding may occur below 20,000 /dL, and life-threatening hemorrhage (e.g., intracranial) typically occurs with counts below 10,000 /dL. It is preferred to give one apheresis unit; 4 to 6 pooled platelet units may also be given.

title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura Evaluation and Management -- Introduction
content: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) was first described in 1924 in a fatal case of a 16-year-old girl who presented with fever, weakness, transient focal neurologic deficits, severe thrombocytopenia, and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. TTP was coined in 1947 based on an autopsy in another fatal case that showed hyaline casts, thrombocytopenia, petechiae, and purpura. [3] This condition was noted to have extremely low survival rates until the 1990s, when therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) was established as the standard of care, which improved survival from 10% to 20% up to 80%.

title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
content: Anti-RhD globulin is immunoglobulin directed against the D-antigen of the Rh blood group. [15] It inhibits the destruction of antibody-coated platelets. Its utility is limited to Rh+ patients with a spleen. The response rate is approximately 70%, with almost all being complete responses (CR), and 26% of these had sustained responses beyond six months. [16] The use of anti-RhD in pregnant women is tempered by the increased risk of neonatal jaundice and anemia. [13]

title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura Evaluation and Management -- Treatment / Management
content: Corticosteroids continue as first-line therapy in conjunction with TPE in the absence of contraindications. The role in acute episodes is related to the autoimmune nature of TTP. The benefits of this intervention include a reduction in the number of sessions of TPE to achieve remission and a reduction of treatment-associated complications. Higher doses are preferred to standard doses because they lead to a higher remission rate.

content: TTP is a rare disease, but severe complications are common even with treatment, and early identification and treatment are associated with improved outcomes. title: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura -- Treatment / Management
content: Other treatments used include splenectomy, cyclosporine, cyclophosphamide, vincristine, and rituximab. [31] [32] [33] These are typically adjunctive agents given when first-line therapy (PEX and corticosteroids) fail. The addition of glucocorticoids and rituximab to PEX has been shown to decrease the required duration of therapeutic plasma exchange. [34] Splenectomy removes a major site of antibody production and complex assimilation. It is a major intervention with results that have been quoted as "variable" in terms of efficacy. Rituximab is an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody found to help treat TTP refractory to PEX. It targets B-lymphocytes and has a good response rate in refractory and relapsed TTP. It has gained more favor than other nonspecific immunosuppressive agents. Vincristine, cyclophosphamide, and cyclosporine are immunosuppressive agents with less than exemplary efficacy. For this reason, they are usually given concurrently with other secondary therapies in refractory cases or when first-line therapy has failed.

title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

content: Mitomycin and mercaptopurine are both contraindicated in diseases of white blood cells, meaning that their use should be avoided in patients who have conditions affecting white blood cell production or function. content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompasses several serious obstetric conditions, specifically hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three entities are classified as hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy, representing major sources of maternal morbidity and mortality. title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.
